---
title: "test_set.Rmd"
output: html_document
date: "2025-06-24"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
library(tidyverse)
library(cowplot)
library(ggforce)
library(ggridges)
options(scipen = 999)
library(colorspace)
library(tidytext)    # for reorder_within() and scale_x_reordered()
```


```{r}
human_neuro_pep <- read.csv(file = "../../data/test_dataset_stats/human_neuro_names.csv")
```

Now get "labeled_bins_save" df from group_2_binned_by_size.Rmd

Let's see if there are any overlaps between the Entries in the two dataset. 
I want to know If I can use the human neuropeptides as my test dataset. 

```{r}
overlap <- inner_join(human_neuro_pep, labeled_bins_save)
```

Now let's remove the common Entries between the two datasets and save this unique list to a csv. 
```{r}
library(dplyr)

# Option 1: anti_join
cleaned_neuropep <- anti_join(
  human_neuro_pep,
  overlap,
  by = "Entry"
)
```
Saving as csv to later pull the fastas from Uniprot:

```{r}
write.csv(cleaned_neuropep, file = "../../data/test_dataset_stats/human_neuro_names_clean.csv", row.names = FALSE)
```
Now I did a pairwise alignement between the original 306 peps I used to find features and my new human neuropeptide test set:

```{r}
alignment_results <- read.csv(file = "../../data/test_dataset_stats/pairwise_alignment_results.csv")

```

Now I am going to remove the peptides that are more than 50% similar to any of the original 306 peps that I used to find features. 

First, I am saving the Entries that have >50% similarity:
```{r}
bad_entries <- alignment_results %>%
  filter(percent_seq_sim > 50.0) 
```

Removing the too close entries from the alignment_results df:
```{r}
alignment_results_filtered <- alignment_results %>%
  filter(!Entry_test %in% c("E9PDJ7", "A0A250SI41"))
```

Now I want to plot a density plot of how similar the remaining test sequences are to the 303 peptides used for training.
```{r}
alignment_results_filtered %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = percent_seq_sim)) +
  geom_density() +
  scale_x_continuous(
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0,0.05)),
    limits = c(0,50)) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0,0))) +
  xlab(label = "percent sequence similarity") +
  theme_cowplot()
```
Now I want to load in the mature peptides and label sites as cleavage sites in each peptide. 
After, we are going to have to sample the positive and negative data. 
First, let's get the mature peptides joined in (with the cleavage sites on the ends)

```{r}
mature_human_peps <- read.csv(file = "../../data/test_dataset_stats/human_mature_pep.csv")
```
Now let's join the two datasets together:
```{r}
mature_human_peps_clean <- mature_human_peps %>%
  rename("Entry" = Uniprot,
         "peptide" = Sequence.mature.peptide.s..plus.cleavage.sites) %>%
  select(Entry, peptide)


joined_human <- alignment_results_filtered %>%
  select(Entry_test, test_seq) %>%
  distinct() %>%
  rename("Entry" = Entry_test,
         "propeptide" = test_seq) %>%
  left_join(mature_human_peps_clean) %>%
  filter(peptide != propeptide) %>% # removing propeptides that are mature peptides
  distinct()
```

```{r}
joined_human %>%
  select(Entry) %>%
  distinct() %>%
  dplyr::count()
```
Here, I am downloading the Uniprot annotations for the 100 entries. 
```{r}
annot_100 <- read.csv(file = "../../data/test_dataset_stats/human_mature_pep_annotations.csv", na.strings = c("", "NA"),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
```

```{r}
human_79 <- joined_human %>%
  select(Entry) %>%
  distinct() 
```

```{r}
filtered_annot <- annot_100 %>%
  inner_join(human_79)
```
```{r}
clean_annot_human <- filtered_annot %>%
  filter(!is.na(Peptide))
```


Now I am labeling the cleavage sites as found in the propeptide:

Locating each peptide in the propeptide:
```{r}
# loc_df <- joined_human %>%
#   # find start/end of the mature peptide and compute propeptide length
#   mutate(
#     loc                  = str_locate(propeptide, peptide),
#     start                = loc[,1],
#     end                  = loc[,2],
#     propeptide_length    = str_length(propeptide)
#   ) %>%
#   select(Entry, propeptide, propeptide_length, start, end, peptide) %>%
#   na.omit() %>%
#   mutate(cleavage_2 = if_else(propeptide_length == end), FALSE, TRUE) %>%
#   mutate(cleavage_1 = if_else())

```

Now I want to manually select peptides that seem to have a correct cleavage site:
```{r}
# list_of_human_peps <- c("P01042", "P01178", "P01189", "P01275", "P01344", "P04808", "P05305", "P06881", "P08949", "P10092", 
#                         "P10645", "P10997", "P12272", "P18509", "P81277", "Q8WXF3", "Q9UHG2", "O15240")
# 
# remove_peps_list <- c("ISLMKRPPGFSPFR",
#                  "WGHEK",
#                  "",
#                  "",
#                  "AYRPSETLCGGELVDTLQFVCGDRGFYFSRPASRVSRRSRGIVEECCFRSCDLALLETYCATPAKSERD",
#                  "CYIQNCPLGGK"
#                  )
# 
# filtered_loc_df <- loc_df %>%
#   filter(Entry %in% list_of_human_peps) %>%
#   mutate(use_end = if_else(propeptide_length == end, FALSE, TRUE)) %>%
#   filter(!peptide %in% remove_peps_list)
```

Let's now save the entries as a fasta file.
```{r}
# for_fasta_human <- filtered_loc_df %>%
#   select(Entry, propeptide) %>%
#   distinct()
# 
# # Create FASTA-formatted lines: ">ID" on one line, followed by the sequence on the next
# fasta_lines_2 <- apply(for_fasta_human, 1, function(row) {
#   paste0(">", row["Entry"], "\n", row["propeptide"])
# })
# 
# # Write these lines to a .fasta file
# writeLines(fasta_lines_2, con = "../../data/group_2_test.fasta")
```

Here I will need to make the group_2_positive_test.csv and group_2_negative_test.csv (sample positions)
```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)
library(tidyr)
library(purrr)

test_labels <- filtered_loc_df %>%
  # 1) collect the 4 cleavage indices per peptide and collapse per propeptide
  group_by(Entry, propeptide) %>%
  summarise(
    cleavage_positions = list(
      unique(
        unlist(
          lapply(seq_along(start), function(i) {
            c(
              start[i],
              start[i] + 1,
              end[i]   - 1,
              end[i]
            )
          })
        )
      )
    ),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  # 2) split into single residues
  mutate(residue = str_split(propeptide, "")) %>%
  unnest_longer(
    residue,
    indices_to = "residue_number",
    values_to  = "residue"
  ) %>%
  # 3) rowwise test
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(
    cleavage_site = residue_number %in% cleavage_positions
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(Entry, residue, residue_number, cleavage_site)

```

Now I need to get the positive and negative samples for the test set. 
```{r}


# Assume your labeled data is in `labeled_df`
# with columns: Entry, residue, residue_number, cleavage_site

# 1) Positive set: all cleavage‐site positions
pos_df <- test_labels %>%
  filter(cleavage_site) %>%
  select(
    Entry,
    residue,
    residue_num = residue_number
  )
```

```{r}
# 2) Count how many positives per Entry
pos_counts <- pos_df %>%
  dplyr::count(Entry, name = "n_pos")
```

```{r}
set.seed(42) 

# 3) Negative set: sample the same number of non‐cleavage sites per Entry
neg_df <- test_labels %>%
  filter(! cleavage_site) %>%
  inner_join(pos_counts, by = "Entry") %>%
  group_by(Entry) %>%
  sample_n(size = first(n_pos)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(
    Entry,
    residue,
    residue_num = residue_number
  )
```

```{r}
# 4) Write out CSVs
write_csv(pos_df, "../../data/concept_groups/sequences/group_2/training/group_2_test_positives.csv")
write_csv(neg_df, "../../data/concept_groups/sequences/group_2/training/group_2_test_negatives.csv")

```


Now I want to look at the pairwise alignment scores within the 303 peptides.
Are there sequences too similar? Is is worth clustering by sequence sim?
```{r}
training_alignment_results <- read.csv(file = "../../data/cleavage_site_sequences_pairwise.csv")
```
Now let's look at the disctribution of sequence similarith in this dataset:

```{r}
training_alignment_results %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = percent_seq_sim)) +
  geom_density() +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0,0))) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0,0))) +
  xlab(label = "percent sequence similarity") +
  theme_cowplot()
```

```{r}
training_alignment_results %>%
  #filter(percent_seq_sim > 40) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = percent_seq_sim)) +
  # color the bars and add a border
  geom_histogram(
    binwidth = 5,
    center   = 2.5,
    fill     = "steelblue",
    color    = "gray40",
    alpha    = 0.7
  ) +
  # add counts above each bar
  geom_text(
    stat      = "bin",
    binwidth  = 5,
    center    = 2.5,
    aes(label = ..count..),
    vjust     = -0.5,
    size      = 4
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05)),
    limits = c(0, 100),
    breaks = seq(0, 100, by = 10)
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))
  ) +
  xlab("percent sequence similarity") +
  theme_cowplot()

```

Now I want to save the top F1 scores to a csv file to use in my linear regression model. 
```{r}
top_scores_hybrid <- winning_F1_modified %>%
  arrange(desc(winning_F1_score)) %>%  # sort so highest F1 is first
  slice_head(n = 2000) %>%               # take the top 100
  mutate(rank = row_number()) %>%       # assign rank 1 to the highest F1
  select(feature, rank)
```

saving to csv:
```{r}
write.csv(top_scores_hybrid, file = "../../data/linear_regression_data/feature_ranking_hybrid.csv", row.names = FALSE)
```










#------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
#-----------  LOGISTIC REGRESSION RESULTS -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
#------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Now here are the classifier results across 6 different homology cutoffs.
```{r}
log_reg_40 <- read.csv(file = "../../data/linear_regression_data/linear_regression_results_40_hom.csv")
log_reg_50 <- read.csv(file = "../../data/linear_regression_data/linear_regression_results_50_hom.csv")
log_reg_60 <- read.csv(file = "../../data/linear_regression_data/linear_regression_results_60_hom.csv")
log_reg_70 <- read.csv(file = "../../data/linear_regression_data/linear_regression_results_70_hom.csv")
log_reg_80 <- read.csv(file = "../../data/linear_regression_data/linear_regression_results_80_hom.csv")
log_reg_90 <- read.csv(file = "../../data/linear_regression_data/linear_regression_results_90_hom.csv")
```

combining data into a single df
```{r}

combined <- bind_rows(
  `40` = log_reg_40,
  `50` = log_reg_50,
  `60` = log_reg_60,
  `70` = log_reg_70,
  `80` = log_reg_80,
  `90` = log_reg_90,
  .id  = "perc_homology"
) %>%
  mutate(
    # make sure perc_homology is numeric, not a string
    perc_homology = as.numeric(perc_homology)
  )
```

Now I want a line plot of validation accuracy across number of features. Each percent homology
will be a line of a different color. 
```{r}

ggplot(combined, aes(x = num_features, y = validation, color = factor(perc_homology))) +
  geom_line(size = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(
    name   = "training/validation\nset homology (%)",
    values = qualitative_hcl(
      n       = length(unique(combined$perc_homology)),
      palette = "Set 2"
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Number of Features",
    y = "Validation Accuracy (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = "right"
  )


```
Now, let's finish building out first test set (human neuropeptides) and then run this logistic regression on our test set and get the result as 
single percent accuracy. 

Test set done (see code above). Remember to go back and improve it. More test sets will be added. 
Done. Let's plot the test results.

```{r}
log_reg_40_test <- read.csv(file = "../../data/linear_regression_data/linear_regression_results_40_hom_test.csv")
log_reg_50_test <- read.csv(file = "../../data/linear_regression_data/linear_regression_results_50_hom_test.csv")
log_reg_60_test <- read.csv(file = "../../data/linear_regression_data/linear_regression_results_60_hom_test.csv")
log_reg_70_test <- read.csv(file = "../../data/linear_regression_data/linear_regression_results_60_hom_test.csv")
log_reg_80_test <- read.csv(file = "../../data/linear_regression_data/linear_regression_results_80_hom_test.csv")
log_reg_90_test <- read.csv(file = "../../data/linear_regression_data/linear_regression_results_90_hom_test.csv")

```

```{r}
combined_test <- bind_rows(
  `40` = log_reg_40_test,
  `50` = log_reg_50_test,
  `60` = log_reg_60_test,
  `70` = log_reg_70_test,
  `80` = log_reg_80_test,
  `90` = log_reg_90_test,
  .id  = "perc_homology"
) %>%
  mutate(
    # make sure perc_homology is numeric, not a string
    perc_homology = as.numeric(perc_homology)
  )
```

```{r}
ggplot(combined_test, aes(x = num_features, y = test_accuracy, color = factor(perc_homology))) +
  geom_line(size = 0.8) +
  scale_color_manual(
    name   = "training/validation\nset homology (%)",
    values = qualitative_hcl(
      n       = length(unique(combined$perc_homology)),
      palette = "Set 2"
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Number of Features",
    y = "Test Accuracy (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = "right"
  )


```
#-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Results from vanilla nn model
#-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

I plotted the results from the last trained models.
```{r}
vanilla_nn_40 <- read.csv(file = "../../data/vanilla_nn_results/nn_results_40_hom.csv")
vanilla_nn_50 <- read.csv(file = "../../data/vanilla_nn_results/nn_results_50_hom.csv")
vanilla_nn_60 <- read.csv(file = "../../data/vanilla_nn_results/nn_results_60_hom.csv")
vanilla_nn_70 <- read.csv(file = "../../data/vanilla_nn_results/nn_results_70_hom.csv")
vanilla_nn_80 <- read.csv(file = "../../data/vanilla_nn_results/nn_results_80_hom.csv")
vanilla_nn_90 <- read.csv(file = "../../data/vanilla_nn_results/nn_results_90_hom.csv")

```


```{r}
combined_nn <- bind_rows(
  `40` = vanilla_nn_40,
  `50` = vanilla_nn_50,
  `60` = vanilla_nn_60,
  `70` = vanilla_nn_70,
  `80` = vanilla_nn_80,
  `90` = vanilla_nn_90,
  .id  = "perc_homology"
) %>%
  mutate(
    # make sure perc_homology is numeric, not a string
    perc_homology = as.numeric(perc_homology)
  )
```

Now let's plot:
```{r}
ggplot(combined_nn, aes(x = num_features, y = validation_accuracy, color = factor(perc_homology))) +
  geom_line(size = 0.8) +
  scale_color_manual(
    name   = "training/validation\nset homology (%)",
    values = qualitative_hcl(
      n       = length(unique(combined$perc_homology)),
      palette = "Set 2"
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Number of Features",
    y = "Validation Accuracy (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = "right"
  )


```

```{r}
ggplot(combined_nn, aes(x = num_features, y = test_accuracy, color = factor(perc_homology))) +
  geom_line(size = 0.8) +
  scale_color_manual(
    name   = "training/validation\nset homology (%)",
    values = qualitative_hcl(
      n       = length(unique(combined$perc_homology)),
      palette = "Set 2"
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Number of Features",
    y = "Test Accuracy (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = "right"
  )


```
Now I want to run the "best" model on the known Novo peptides. 
Let's start with the nn model. I need a script that will take the embeddings, load the model and generate the prediction probabilities for each of these peptides.

```{r}
# this model is not impressive- skip for now
```

Now let's look at the log regression model predictions of Novo-familiar peptides:
```{r}
log_reg_predictions <- read.csv(file = "../../data/linear_regression_data/example_predictions_logreg_hom90_600_new.csv")
```

making a plot for each of the 4 Entries:
```{r echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

library(ggiraph)
library(htmltools)

# your predictions data frame
# columns: Entry, position, residue, probability_cleavage_site
# assume it's already loaded as `log_reg_predictions`

entries <- c("A0A1B0GTK4", "A0A1B0GVD1", "P01189", "P01275")

plot_labels <- c("A0A1B0GTK4 - FAM237A", "A0A1B0GVD1 - FAM237B", "P01189 - POMC", "P01275 - glucagon")

# build one girafe plot per Entry
plots <- lapply(entries, function(entry) {
  df <- log_reg_predictions %>% filter(Entry == entry)
  
  p <- ggplot(df, aes(x = position, y = probability_cleavage_site)) +
    geom_col_interactive(
      aes(tooltip = paste0(
        "Residue: ", residue,
        "\nPos: ", position,
        "\nProb: ", sprintf("%.2f", probability_cleavage_site)
      )),
      fill = "steelblue"
    ) +
    scale_x_continuous(
      breaks = df$position,
      labels = df$residue,
      expand = expansion(mult = c(0,0))
    ) +
    scale_y_continuous(
      expand = expansion(mult = c(0,0))
    ) +
    labs(
      title = entry,
      x     = "",
      y     = "Prob (cleavage site)"
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(
      axis.text.x = element_text(vjust = 0.5, size = 5),
      plot.title  = element_text(hjust = 0.5)
    )
  
  girafe(
    ggobj      = p,
    width_svg  = 9.5,
    height_svg = 2,
    options = list(
      opts_hover(css    = "fill:#FF3333;"),
      opts_tooltip(offx = 10, offy = -10),
      opts_sizing(rescale = TRUE)
    )
  )
})

# stack them vertically and save to HTML
page <- tagList(
  lapply(plots, function(g) {
    tags$div(style="margin-bottom: 20px;", g)
  })
)

save_html(page, file = "../../data/linear_regression_data/predictions_interactive_new.html")

```



I want to add in the cleavage sites. For this, I downloaded the 4 peptides from uniprot. 
```{r}
four_novo_annotations <- read.csv(file = "../../src/bash/uniprot_data.tsv", sep = "\t")
```


Note: get "clean_data_5" in clean_KW-0527.Rmd
```{r}
four_novo_annotations <- four_novo_annotations %>%
  select(Entry, Sequence, Length, Peptide, Signal.peptide) %>%
  distinct() %>%
  na.omit(Peptide) 
```


Here is a list of all the peptide starts and ends. 
```{r}
four_novo_ann_start_end <- four_novo_annotations %>%
  # extract all “123..456” substrings as a list-column
  mutate(range_str = str_extract_all(Peptide, "\\d+\\.\\.\\d+")) %>%
  # turn each element of that list into its own row
  unnest_longer(range_str) %>%
  # split “123..456” into two new columns
  separate(range_str, into = c("peptide_start","peptide_end"), sep = "\\.\\.") %>%
  # convert to integer
  mutate(
     start = as.integer(peptide_start),
     end   = as.integer(peptide_end)) %>%
  select(Entry, Sequence, Length, peptide_start, peptide_end)

```

get Signal sequence info: (where signal starts and ends so that later we can remove cleavage site right after signal)
```{r}
signal_info_novo <- four_novo_annotations %>%
  select(Entry, Signal.peptide) %>%
  distinct() %>%
  mutate(range_str = str_extract_all(Signal.peptide, "\\d+\\.\\.\\d+")) %>%
  unnest_longer(range_str) %>%
  separate(range_str, into = c("signal_start","signal_end"), sep = "\\.\\.") %>%
  mutate(
     start = as.integer(signal_start),
     end   = as.integer(signal_end)) %>%
  select(Entry, signal_start, signal_end)
```

Joining the peptide and signal info dataframes together.

```{r}
two_pep_novo <- four_novo_ann_start_end %>%
  left_join(signal_info_novo)
```

```{r}
right_after_signal_novo <- two_pep_novo %>%
  mutate(peptide_after_signal = ifelse(as.numeric(signal_end) + 1 == peptide_start, TRUE, FALSE))
```  
  
Let's see how many peptides are NOT right after the signal:
```{r}
right_after_signal_novo %>%
  filter(peptide_after_signal == FALSE) %>%
  dplyr::count()

```
```{r}
final_cleavage_site_df_novo <- right_after_signal_novo %>%
  filter(!(peptide_start == 1 & peptide_end == Length)) # filtering out mature peptides
```

Getting the positive data labels for the cleavage sites
```{r}
library(stringr)

positive_labels_novo <- final_cleavage_site_df_novo %>%
  # for each row, build a list of the 4 flanking positions
  rowwise() %>%
    mutate(
      # build a tiny tibble of flank positions + their labels
      flank = list(tibble::tibble(
        residue_number = c(as.numeric(peptide_start) - 2,
                           as.numeric(peptide_start) - 1,
                           as.numeric(peptide_end)   + 1,
                           as.numeric(peptide_end)   + 2),
        cleavage       = c("cleavage_1",   
                           "cleavage_1",
                           "cleavage_2",
                           "cleavage_2"),
        cleavage_pos       = c("first\nouter",   
                           "first\ninner",
                           "second\ninner",
                           "second\nouter")
      ))
    ) %>%
    unnest(cols = c(flank)) %>% 
    # drop any cleavage_1 when start == 1 or after_signal == TRUE
    filter(!(cleavage == "cleavage_1" & (peptide_start == 1 | peptide_after_signal))) %>%
    # drop any cleavage_2 when end == Length
    filter(!(cleavage == "cleavage_2" & peptide_end == Length)) %>%
    # also drop any positions that fall outside 1…Length
    filter(residue_number >= 1, residue_number <= Length) %>%
    # extract the amino acid at each flank position
    mutate(residue = str_sub(Sequence, residue_number, residue_number))
    
positive_labels_novo <- positive_labels_novo %>%
  select(Entry, Sequence, Length, residue, residue_number) %>%
  distinct()

positive_labels_novo <- positive_labels_novo %>%
  select(Entry, residue, residue_number) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  distinct()
```

```{r}
library(ggiraph)
library(htmltools)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

# assumed: log_reg_predictions and positive_labels_novo are already loaded

entries <- c("A0A1B0GTK4", "A0A1B0GVD1", "P01189", "P01275")
plot_labels <- c("A0A1B0GTK4 - FAM237A", "A0A1B0GVD1 - FAM237B", "P01189 - POMC", "P01275 - glucagon")

# build one girafe plot per Entry
plots <- lapply(seq_along(entries), function(i) {
  entry <- entries[i]
  label <- plot_labels[i]

  df <- log_reg_predictions %>% filter(Entry == entry)
  highlights <- positive_labels_novo %>%
    filter(Entry == entry) %>%
    pull(residue_number)

  p <- ggplot(df, aes(x = position, y = probability_cleavage_site)) +
    # highlight cleavage sites in neon green
    geom_vline(
      xintercept = highlights,
      color = "#39FF14",          # neon green
      linewidth = 0.9,            # match blue bar width
      alpha = 0.9
    ) +
    # interactive bars
    geom_col_interactive(
      aes(tooltip = paste0(
        "Residue: ", residue,
        "\nPos: ", position,
        "\nProb: ", sprintf("%.2f", probability_cleavage_site)
      )),
      fill = "steelblue"
    ) +
    scale_x_continuous(
      breaks = df$position,
      labels = df$residue,
      expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0))
    ) +
    scale_y_continuous(
      expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0))
    ) +
    labs(
      title = label,
      x = "",
      y = "Prob (cleavage site)"
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(
      axis.text.x = element_text(vjust = 0.5, size = 5),
      plot.title  = element_text(hjust = 0.5)
    )

  girafe(
    ggobj = p,
    width_svg = 9.5,
    height_svg = 2,
    options = list(
      opts_hover(css = "fill:#FF3333;"),
      opts_tooltip(offx = 10, offy = -10),
      opts_sizing(rescale = TRUE)
    )
  )
})

# stack and save as HTML
page <- tagList(
  lapply(plots, function(g) {
    tags$div(style = "margin-bottom: 20px;", g)
  })
)

save_html(page, file = "../../data/linear_regression_data/predictions_interactive_new_90_600.html")

```
